title: Antirhea coriacea
taxonomy: Antirhea coriacea is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Rubiaceae. This family includes approximately 650 genera and 13,000 species, many of which are economically important for their medicinal and timber values. The Rubiaceae family is a large and diverse group of flowering plants, commonly known as the coffee or madder family. Antirhea coriacea is a member of the Rubiaceae family and is native to the tropical forests of Central and South America. The classification of Antirhea coriacea is further divided into more specific categories, including genus, species, and infraspecific taxonomic ranks.
uses: Antirhea coriacea is a plant species that has been used in traditional medicine, particularly in the Caribbean region. Its medicinal uses include treating various ailments such as fever, rheumatism, and digestive issues. The plant's extracts have anti-inflammatory properties, which help to reduce pain and swelling. Antirhea coriacea is also used to treat skin conditions and wounds due to its antibacterial properties. Further research is needed to fully understand the plant's medicinal potential and its effectiveness in treating various health conditions.

intro: Antirhea coriacea, also known as the "Cassitera tree," is a species of flowering plant in the family Rubiaceae, native to the tropical regions of Central and South America. The plant has been traditionally used to treat various health issues, including fever, inflammation, and gastrointestinal problems, due to its potential health benefits. The bioactive constituents of Antirhea coriacea include alkaloids, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with Antirhea coriacea include teas, infusions, and extracts, often used to support immune function and alleviate symptoms of colds and flu.
